chain_of_thought: Top native frame below allocator/sanitizer is sub70 in libaoecore.so. Ghidra MCP calls are currently erroring (404) so I cannot decompile sub70 or inspect its callers/arguments. I therefore cannot see whether this is a memcpy-style bug, a use-after-free, or a logic bug in sub70; I only see that scudo reports header corruption on deallocate, which is consistent with heap corruption in application code or with harness abusing the allocator (double free, freeing foreign pointers, overwriting metadata, etc.).

The stack shows fuzz_one_input -> sub70, not a normal JNI entry. The JNI bridge method Java_com_google_android_decode_AoeUtils_ae is mentioned in the crash metadata, but the native stack here clearly comes from a standalone fuzz harness with main/fuzz_one_input and not from the Android runtime. That strongly suggests this execution is under a custom fuzzer that directly calls into libaoecore.so, not via the real app/ART, and may be feeding arbitrary bytes or misusing APIs in ways production code does not (e.g., passing bogus pointers, random sizes).

Because I cannot inspect sub70, I cannot demonstrate that it takes attacker-controlled input (from a realistic Java path) and uses it unsafely. The only concrete taint chain I have is fuzz_one_input (harness) -> sub70, where fuzz_one_input is intentionally adversarial and possibly violating preconditions. There is no visible JNI frame or Java call chain on this stack, and no evidence that Java_com_google_android_decode_AoeUtils_ae passes unvalidated user input into the dangerous path.

Scudo header corruption on free indicates heap metadata corruption, which is generally a strong sign of a memory safety issue somewhere. However, without visibility into the code or its calling conventions, it is equally possible that the harness is freeing a buffer twice or freeing memory not allocated by scudo (common in fuzz shims), which scudo correctly aborts on. The presence of dedicated fuzz_one_input and main strongly supports a synthetic fuzz environment rather than the real app control flow.

Given these constraints, I cannot confidently attribute the crash to a genuine, app-reachable vulnerability. The more conservative classification, consistent with the definition, is to treat this as an environment/harness-induced heap misuse until there is evidence that the same pattern is reachable from Java input along a real JNI path with attacker-controlled data.
reasons: Crash occurs under a standalone fuzz harness (fuzz_one_input -> main) rather than normal JNI/Java execution
Scudo header corruption on deallocate could be caused by harness misuse (double free, freeing invalid pointers) rather than a bug in app logic
Ghidra MCP endpoints failed (404), so sub70 implementation and its argument sources cannot be inspected, preventing proof of unsafe use of attacker-controlled data
No Java call graph or JNI-level path is visible in the crashing stack; JNI bridge is only mentioned in metadata, not observed on the stack
recommendations: Re-run fuzzing with an instrumented build where sub70 can be decompiled and inspected to locate the exact heap misuse
Add assertions or debug logging in sub70 and fuzz_one_input around allocations/frees to detect double frees or size mismatches
Attempt to reproduce similar behavior through the real JNI bridge Java_com_google_android_decode_AoeUtils_ae using realistic Java inputs to determine app-reachability
assumptions: fuzz_one_input is a custom harness not present in production builds
The provided stack trace is complete and correctly ordered
No additional sanitizer output or source code is available beyond the crash metadata
limitations: Unable to decompile or inspect sub70 due to tool 404 errors
No Java call graph or actual JNI invocation path tied to this crash was provided
Cannot see how fuzz_one_input constructs its inputs or whether it violates documented API preconditions
